subUnits.title: 12.1 Plateaus: Meaning, Types, Uses & Disadvantages
notes: Definition of Plateau:
• Elevated flat-topped landform with steep sides (escarpments)
• Also called tableland or highland
• Elevation: typically >300m above surrounding areas
• Formed by tectonic uplift, volcanic activity, or erosion
Types of Plateaus:
1. Tectonic/Structural Plateaus:
• Uplifted by crustal movements without folding
• Examples: Colorado Plateau (USA), Tibetan Plateau, Jos Plateau
2. Volcanic Plateaus:
• Built by successive lava flows covering large areas
• Examples: Deccan Plateau (India), Columbia Plateau (USA)
3. Dissected Plateaus:
• Former plateaus heavily eroded by rivers/glaciers
• Rugged terrain with deep valleys
• Examples: Appalachian Plateau, Nigerian Eastern Highlands
4. Intermontane Plateaus:
• Surrounded by mountains on all sides
• Examples: Tibetan Plateau, Altiplano (Andes)
Characteristics:
• Flat or gently rolling summit
• Steep escarpments on margins
• Often contain rivers, lakes, mineral deposits
• Cooler climate than surrounding lowlands
Uses/Importance of Plateaus:
• Agriculture: cool climate suits temperate crops (tea, coffee, potatoes, maize)
• Mining: rich in minerals (tin, coal, limestone, gold)
• Water supply: source of rivers, hydroelectric power potential
• Tourism: scenic views, national parks, cultural sites
• Settlement: moderate climate, defense advantages
Disadvantages:
• Soil erosion on steep escarpments
• Difficult transport and communication networks
• Limited arable land on rocky surfaces
• Weather extremes: frost, high winds, thin soils
• Isolation from economic centers
Nigerian Examples:
• Jos Plateau: tin mining, cool climate, tourism (Shere Hills, Assop Falls)
• Udi/Nsukka Plateau: coal deposits, agricultural activities
• Mambilla Plateau: highest in Nigeria, hydroelectric potential, tea farming

quiz.q: A plateau is best described as:
opts: Elevated flat-topped land with steep sides
quiz.explanation: Plateaus are elevated tablelands with relatively flat summits and steep escarpments.

quiz.q: The Jos Plateau in Nigeria is primarily a:
quiz.explanation: Jos Plateau formed by crustal uplift of basement complex rocks, classifying it as tectonic.

quiz.q: A major agricultural advantage of plateaus is:
opts: Cool climate suitable for temperate crops
quiz.explanation: Higher elevation creates cooler temperatures ideal for crops like tea, coffee, potatoes, and maize.

quiz.q: Which mineral is famously mined on the Jos Plateau?
quiz.explanation: Jos Plateau is Nigeria's historic tin and columbite mining center, with extensive alluvial and primary deposits.

quiz.q: A disadvantage of plateaus is:
quiz.explanation: Steep margins make road/rail construction challenging and increase erosion risk.

quiz.q: Intermontane plateaus are:
quiz.explanation: Intermontane plateaus are enclosed by mountain ranges, like the Tibetan Plateau and Altiplano.

quiz.q: Mambilla Plateau is important for:
quiz.explanation: Mambilla's high elevation supports hydropower projects and cool-climate agriculture like tea.

quiz.q: Dissected plateaus are formed by:
quiz.explanation: Rivers and glaciers cut into former plateaus, creating rugged, valley-dissected terrain.

quiz.q: Plateaus contribute to water supply by:
opts: Serving as river sources and catchment areas
quiz.explanation: Elevated plateaus receive orographic rainfall, feeding rivers and providing hydroelectric potential.

quiz.q: Which is NOT a typical plateau characteristic?
quiz.explanation: Conical peaks are volcanic mountains; plateaus have flat or gently rolling summits with steep sides.

subUnits.title: 12.2 Lowlands: Meaning, Types, Formation, Importance & Disadvantages
notes: Definition of Lowlands:
• Gently sloping or flat areas generally below 200m elevation
• Also called plains, valleys, or basins
• Formed by deposition, erosion, tectonic subsidence, or glacial action
Types of Lowlands:
1. Coastal Plains:
• Flat areas near oceans, formed by marine deposition
• Examples: Niger Delta, Gulf Coast plains
• Features: beaches, lagoons, mangroves, estuaries
2. Interior Plains:
• Large flat areas inland, often sedimentary
• Examples: Great Plains (USA), Sokoto Plains, Chad Basin
• Features: fertile soils, agriculture, grasslands
3. Valleys:
• Low areas between hills/mountains
• Types: V-shaped (river erosion), U-shaped (glacial), Rift valleys (tectonic)
• Examples: Benue Valley, East African Rift Valley
4. Depressions/Basins:
• Lowest areas in a region, often collecting water
• Examples: Chad Basin, Congo Basin, Qattara Depression
Formation Processes:
• River deposition (alluvial plains)
• Marine transgression and sedimentation
• Tectonic downfaulting (rift valleys)
• Glacial scouring and deposition
• Wind erosion and deposition (desert plains)
Importance/Uses of Lowlands:
• Agriculture: deep fertile soils, mechanization, large-scale farming
• Settlement: easy construction, transport networks, urban growth
• Industry: flat land for factories, ports, infrastructure
• Water resources: rivers, lakes, groundwater recharge
• Biodiversity: wetlands, floodplains, rich ecosystems
Disadvantages:
• Flooding during heavy rains or river overflow
• Waterlogging and poor drainage in some areas
• Disease vectors: malaria, schistosomiasis in stagnant water
• Limited scenic/tourism value compared to highlands
• Soil leaching in high rainfall areas reduces fertility
Nigerian Examples:
• Niger Delta: oil/gas, mangroves, fishing, flooding challenges
• Sokoto/Rima Plains: cereal farming, irrigation, pastoralism
• Benue Valley: rice cultivation, transport corridor, seasonal floods
• Chad Basin: Lake Chad recession, agriculture, cross-border trade

quiz.q: Lowlands are generally defined as areas below:
quiz.explanation: Geographers classify lowlands as gently sloping or flat terrain below 200m above sea level.

quiz.q: Coastal plains are primarily formed by:
quiz.explanation: Coastal plains form from accumulated sediments deposited by rivers and ocean currents near shorelines.

quiz.q: The Niger Delta is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Niger Delta is a coastal lowland formed by massive sediment deposition from the Niger River into the Atlantic.

quiz.q: A major agricultural advantage of lowlands is:
opts: Deep fertile soils suitable for mechanization
quiz.explanation: Lowlands accumulate rich alluvial soils, enabling large-scale mechanized farming and high crop yields.

quiz.q: Which is a common disadvantage of lowlands?
quiz.explanation: Low elevation and poor drainage make lowlands prone to flooding, especially during heavy rains.

quiz.q: V-shaped valleys are formed primarily by:
quiz.explanation: Fast-flowing rivers erode vertically, creating steep-sided V-shaped valleys in upland areas.

quiz.q: The Benue Valley is important for:
quiz.explanation: Benue Valley's alluvial soils support rice farming, and the river serves as a transport and irrigation corridor.

quiz.q: Rift valleys are formed by:
quiz.explanation: Tensional forces pull crust apart, dropping central blocks to form linear rift valleys (e.g., East African Rift).

quiz.q: Lowlands often face disease challenges due to:
opts: Stagnant water breeding mosquitoes and parasites
quiz.explanation: Poor drainage creates breeding grounds for malaria vectors and waterborne parasites like schistosomiasis.

quiz.q: Which Nigerian lowland is known for cereal farming and pastoralism?
quiz.explanation: Sokoto and Rima plains feature fertile soils, irrigation potential, and extensive grain farming and cattle rearing.
